front: What are the two main methods used for parameter estimation in ARIMA models?
back: The two main methods are: 1) Least Squares Estimation - minimizes the sum of squared residuals, and 2) Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) - finds parameter values that maximize the likelihood function for the observed data.

front: What is the key principle behind Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for time series parameters?
back: MLE selects the parameter values that make the observed data most probable. It finds the parameters that maximize the joint probability density function (the likelihood function) of the observed time series.

front: What is a residual in the context of estimating an ARMA(p, q) model?
back: A residual (ε̂_t) is the difference between the observed value (Y_t) and the model's predicted value (Ŷ_t). It represents the unexplained portion of the time series after the AR and MA components have been accounted for: ε̂_t = Y_t - (φ₁Y_{t-1} + ... + φ_pY_{t-p} + θ₁ε_{t-1} + ... + θ_qε_{t-q}).

front: Why is it necessary for an AR process to be stationary for parameter estimation?
back: Stationarity (specifically, weak stationarity) is a fundamental assumption for most estimation techniques. It ensures that the mean, variance, and autocovariance structure of the process are constant over time, which is required for the properties of estimators (like consistency and asymptotic normality) to hold.

front: What is the purpose of diagnostic checking after parameter estimation?
back: Diagnostic checking is used to validate the fitted model. It involves analyzing the residuals to see if they resemble white noise (no autocorrelation) and ensuring the estimated parameters are statistically significant. This confirms whether the model has adequately captured the underlying structure of the time series.
